item.title: A common reason Express Entry candidates miss their ITA deadline, and how to avoid it
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Express Entry, ITA, permanent residence, police certificates, documentation

Summary: When an Express Entry candidate receives an Invitation to Apply (ITA), they have a strict timeline of 60 days to submit a complete application for permanent residence (PR). This application must be thorough and include all required documentation. Among these documents, police certificates are particularly critical yet can often take an extended period to obtain. If candidates do not secure their police certificates in time, they risk jeopardizing their PR application. It is essential for candidates to start gathering these documents early and to understand the processing times involved. Failing to meet the 60-day deadline can result in the rejection of the application altogether. Consequently, candidates are advised to prioritize obtaining their police certificates and other necessary documents as soon as they receive their ITA. Being proactive in this regard can help ensure a smooth application process and minimize the risk of delays or refusal of their permanent residence application.

Once an Express Entry candidate receives an invitation to apply (ITA), they must submit a complete application for permanent residence (PR) within 60 days, with all required documentation included. Police certificates in particular can take a long time to obtain, and failure to do so before the 60-day countdown runs out can put your PR […]

item.title: Express Entry Draw #421 | Second-Ever Physicians with Canadian Work Experience Draw
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Express Entry, Draw #421, ITAs, physicians, Canadian work experience

Summary: The recent Express Entry Draw #421 has marked a significant milestone by issuing 271 Invitations to Apply (ITAs) exclusively to physicians who have experience working in Canada. This is only the second time such a targeted draw has been conducted, highlighting the importance of healthcare professionals in the Canadian immigration system. The move is indicative of the Canadian government’s ongoing efforts to address labor shortages in the healthcare sector, particularly following the challenges posed by the COVID-19 pandemic. Physicians who received ITAs in this draw are likely to have a quicker path to permanent residency as they possess both the qualifications and relevant work experience needed in Canadian healthcare settings. The decision to focus on this group aims to streamline the immigration process for those who can contribute significantly to the wellbeing of Canadians. Overall, this targeted approach shows the government’s recognition of the critical role that physicians play in supporting public health and the broader economy, fostering a more efficient immigration pathway for these essential workers.
Express Entry Draw #421 issued 271 Invitations to Apply (ITAs) for the second time ever exclusively to physicians with Canadian work experience.

item.title: Express Entry Draw #422 | 4,000 Healthcare and Social Service Professionals Invited Again!
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Express Entry, Canada, permanent residency, point-based system, immigration

Summary: Express Entry is a system implemented by the Canadian government to streamline the process of managing applications for Canadian permanent residency. It operates on a point-based system, where candidates are assessed based on various factors including age, education, work experience, and language proficiency. The highest-scoring candidates in the Comprehensive Ranking System (CRS) are invited to apply for permanent residency during regular draws conducted by Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC). The program is designed to attract skilled workers to Canada, filling labor shortages and contributing to the economy. Applicants must first create an online profile, after which they receive a score based on their credentials. Various factors can enhance a candidate's score, such as securing a job offer from a Canadian employer or obtaining a provincial nomination. Express Entry encompasses several immigration programs, including the Federal Skilled Worker Program, Federal Skilled Trades Program, and Canadian Experience Class. It offers a fast-track route to permanent residency, significantly reducing processing times compared to traditional application methods, thus making Canada an appealing destination for potential immigrants.
Express Entry is a system used by the Canadian government to manage applications for Canadian permanent residency. Express Entry uses a point-based ...

item.title: How To Prepare For Saskatchewan's Next SINP Intake Window In July - Immigration.ca
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Express Entry, ITAs, Provincial Nominee Program, allocations, reform

Summary: Provinces across Canada are pressing the federal government, specifically Ottawa, to increase the allocations for the Provincial Nominee Program (PNP) and to reform the Express Entry system. The PNP allows provinces to select immigrants who meet their specific labor market needs, and it has become a crucial pathway for many who wish to settle in Canada. However, the current allocation limits are seen as restrictive, hindering provinces from fully utilizing this program to address local workforce shortages. Advocates argue that increasing the number of nominations would not only benefit the provinces but also the national economy by attracting skilled labor. Moreover, there is a call for a reform of the Express Entry system to make it more responsive to the changing demographics and economic demands of various regions across Canada. Stakeholders believe that both increasing allocations and reforming the system would enhance Canada's immigration strategy, making it more equitable and effective. Dialogue between provincial representatives and federal authorities is ongoing, reflecting the urgency of improving the PNP and Express Entry for future immigration planning.

item.title: Manitoba conducted a new invitation round under the Skilled Worker Stream - LPEN
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: LAAs, Express Entry, MPNP, invitations, candidates

Summary: A total of 77 Letters of Advice to Apply (LAAs) were issued recently. Out of these, 15 LAAs were specifically allocated to candidates who possess valid Express Entry profiles and have a job seeker validation code. This highlights the continued focus on integrating skilled workers into the labor market through Canada's Express Entry system. Additionally, the cumulative number of invitations issued through the Manitoba Provincial Nominee Program (MPNP) has now reached 1,910. This statistic reflects the ongoing efforts of the MPNP to attract and select qualified individuals to address labor shortages in Manitoba. The inclusion of candidates with Express Entry profiles ensures that the province is able to draw from a wider pool of skilled labor, thus enhancing its economic growth and meeting local demands. These numbers indicate a strategic approach to immigration, aligning with both provincial needs and the broader federal immigration goals. As the demand for skilled workers continues to rise, the issuance of LAAs remains an essential mechanism in Canada's immigration framework, fostering a diverse and capable workforce while supporting the province's long-term socio-economic plans.

As Canada continues to evolve its immigration policies, these provincial initiatives complement federal efforts by addressing localized challenges and opportunities in the labor market. Overall, the collaboration between provincial and federal strategies illustrates Canada's commitment to sustaining economic growth through diverse immigration channels while aligning with broader national objectives. By balancing skilled labor needs and entrepreneurship, Canada's immigration landscape continues to adapt in a way that benefits both immigrants and the Canadian economy.

item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Canada, healthcare, settling, community, finances

Summary: Moving to Canada involves many tasks, including finding housing, setting up finances, and integrating into your new community. Healthcare, while essential, might not be your top priority initially. It's important to understand that Canada's healthcare system is different from those in other countries. This understanding can significantly affect your overall settlement experience. Familiarizing yourself with the healthcare services available in your province or territory should be an essential part of your settling-in process. It’s advisable to research how to access medical services, obtain a health card, and learn about the coverage you will receive. Many provinces offer a public healthcare system that covers basic medical services, but private insurance may also be necessary for certain aspects of care, particularly in terms of wait times and specific services. As you get accustomed to your new life, don't overlook the importance of connecting with local healthcare providers and understanding your entitlements. Getting a grasp on healthcare early on will help ensure that you and your family can receive the care you need as you transition into your new environment.

item.title: Temporary residence wait times fall in latest IRCC processing update
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Immigration, Canada, work permit, processing times, applicants

Summary: On July 2, Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) announced updated processing times for temporary residence applications, highlighting positive developments for work permit applicants. For in-Canada applicants, work permit processing times have significantly improved, with a reduction of an additional 15 days, reaching their lowest level this year. This improvement reflects the Canadian government's ongoing efforts to streamline the application process and reduce wait times for applicants seeking temporary residency. Furthermore, applicants from Nigeria also experienced a one-week enhancement in their processing times, showcasing a targeted effort to address specific regional challenges. These changes are part of a broader commitment by the IRCC to make Canada's immigration system more efficient and responsive to the needs of prospective immigrants. The easing of processing times is likely to encourage more individuals to consider Canada as a destination for work opportunities, enhancing the country's labor market and contributing positively to its economy. This update marks a significant step forward in improving the overall experience for temporary residents, aligning with Canada’s goal of attracting diverse talent from around the world.

On July 2, Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) published updated processing times for various temporary residence applications, with good news for work permit applicants. item.title: How Canada’s citizenship by descent law helped one footballer make Canada’s FIFA World Cup squad
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Alfie Jones, Canadian citizen, national soccer team, citizenship laws, grandmother

Summary: Alfie Jones, an England-born football player, recently became a Canadian citizen, which enabled him to join Canada’s national soccer team. His eligibility for citizenship stems from his grandmother, who was born in Hillcrest, Alberta. Until last year, Canada’s laws limited citizenship by descent, making it impossible for individuals with only a grandparent born in Canada to obtain citizenship. This situation changed with a revision in the citizenship laws, allowing individuals with a Canadian grandparent to acquire citizenship. Jones’ situation highlights the significance of these changes, as they open doors for many individuals with Canadian heritage to represent Canada on international platforms. Grants of citizenship through descent have become increasingly relevant, reflecting Canada’s commitment to welcoming individuals connected to its history. Jones’s journey illustrates the intersection of sports and national identity, as he embarks on this new chapter in his career, proudly representing Canada in soccer. His story resonates with many who aspire to connect with their roots and contribute to Canada’s diverse sporting culture.

England-born football player Alfie Jones became a Canadian citizen last year, and earned a place on Canada’s national soccer team – all because of a grandmother born in Hillcrest, Alberta. For around 16 years, having a Canadian grandparent wasn’t enough to pass citizenship down the family line. But Canada’s changed its citizenship by descent laws […]

The post How Canada’s citizenship by descent law helped one footballer make Canada’s FIFA World Cup squad first appeared on CIC News.

item.title: Can a Representative Complete the OINP Employer Portal Process? - YouTube
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Ontario, PR, OINP, Employer Pathway, Immigration

Summary: The Ontario Immigrant Nominee Program (OINP) provides pathways for individuals to obtain Permanent Residency (PR) in Ontario without relying on Express Entry. One of the key pathways under the OINP is the Employer Pathway, which allows employers to nominate foreign workers for PR based on their skills and experience. To qualify, candidates must have a permanent job offer from an Ontario employer, along with relevant work experience in their field. It is crucial for applicants to ensure that their job offer meets the OINP requirements, such as being in a skilled occupation and having a salary that aligns with industry standards. Additionally, candidates should prepare for the application process by gathering necessary documentation, including proof of work experience and educational credentials. The OINP may also require candidates to demonstrate their intent to reside in Ontario. Overall, the OINP Employer Pathway offers a valuable alternative route to PR for individuals who may not be eligible for Express Entry, especially those already working in Ontario or with skills that are in demand within the province.

item.title: Millions of Americans have two independence days this year. Most don’t know it
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Canada Day, independence, citizenship, flags, legal fact

Summary: In the week marking July 1, Canada Day, and July 4, Independence Day, two flags are raised, symbolizing national pride. Recently, Canada has implemented new citizenship policies allowing American citizens to hold dual citizenship. This legal development means that millions of Americans may now own Canadian citizenship as well, without being fully aware of it. The announcement is significant, as it encourages dual nationality and strengthens ties between the two neighboring countries. It highlights the rich cultural exchange and connection that exists between Canada and the United States. The change in citizenship laws accommodates those who have historically had ties to Canada, such as family connections or long-standing residency. Many Americans who own both flags are encouraged to explore this newfound recognition of their Canadian identity, boosting the notion of dual citizenship. This legal fact not only opens doors for Americans in terms of residency and benefits but also fosters greater mutual understanding and appreciation between the citizens of both nations. As both holidays approach, the celebration of identity and belonging takes center stage, reminding individuals of the privileges and connections that come with citizenship.

Two flags go up this week, three days apart. Canada marks Canada Day on July 1, and the United States celebrates its independence on July 4. All American citizens own the latter, but millions of them own both and haven’t found out yet. This is a legal fact, not a metaphor. Under Canada’s new citizenship […]

item.title: US to deny entry to foreign pregnant women after SC verdict on birthright citizenship?
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: US, foreign pregnant women, entry denial, birthright citizenship, Supreme Court

Summary: The United States has announced plans to deny entry to foreign pregnant women following a recent Supreme Court verdict concerning birthright citizenship. This policy change is aimed at preventing individuals from traveling to the U.S. to give birth and thus secure citizenship for their children. The ruling reflects ongoing debates around immigration and the rights conferred by birthright citizenship as outlined in the 14th Amendment. The administration argues that this move is necessary to uphold immigration laws and manage resource allocations effectively. Critics of the policy contend that it discriminates against pregnant women and undermines family unity. Additionally, there are concerns about the implications for international relations, as the policy may deter legitimate travelers. This significant shift in immigration policy may lead to increased scrutiny and enforcement at points of entry, impacting not only pregnant travelers but also those accompanying them. Legal experts are evaluating potential challenges to the policy, suggesting that it could face opposition in the courts. Overall, the decision adds another layer to the complex landscape of U.S. immigration policy and the contentious discourse surrounding citizenship rights.

item.content:encoded:

Summary: The province of Saskatchewan has issued a total of 2,628 nominations for provincial immigration in the current year, which accounts for approximately 55% of its 2026 allocation. This update is based on information published on the province’s official website. The total allocation for provincial nominations under the Saskatchewan Immigrant Nominee Program (SINP) is set at 4,761 spaces. This figure remains consistent with the province's finalized quota for the upcoming year, 2025. The SINP allows employers to nominate foreign workers and international students who wish to immigrate to Saskatchewan and contribute to its economy. With a focus on attracting skilled labor and addressing labor shortages within the province, the nominations and allocations play a crucial role in shaping Saskatchewan's immigration strategy. Potential candidates for the SINP should assess their eligibility against the program's criteria, which may consider factors such as work experience, education, and language proficiency. Staying informed about the nomination process and allocation numbers can help applicants better navigate their immigration journey to Saskatchewan.

item.title: BREAKING: IRCC claims only 1% of citizenship by descent applicants impacted, in first official statement on certificate surrenders
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Immigration, Citizenship, Bill C-3, IRCC, eligibility

Summary: Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) has provided an initial detailed statement regarding the review of citizenship certificates issued under Bill C-3, which was introduced to address certain citizenship issues. Initially, the IRCC identified approximately 100 certificates that raised potential eligibility concerns. Out of these flagged cases, the department has automatically reinstated 33 certificates after confirming that those individuals met the legal requirements for citizenship. This decision reflects the IRCC's commitment to resolving citizenship statuses in alignment with existing laws. The review is currently ongoing for the remaining 67 cases, which may involve further scrutiny to determine their eligibility for citizenship. The department's proactive approach aims to ensure that individuals who rightfully qualify for citizenship are recognized and that any discrepancies are addressed appropriately. IRCC’s transparency in communicating these developments serves to reassure those affected, as well as the broader public, about the integrity of the citizenship issuance process under the provisions of Bill C-3. This update is crucial for maintaining confidence in Canada’s immigration and citizenship system during the course of the review.

Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) has released its first detailed statement on the review of citizenship certificates issued under Bill C-3, saying it initially flagged just 100 certificates with potential eligibility issues. This year marks a significant change, as 1947 was when Canada first established citizenship as a formal legal status, outlining clear criteria for who would obtain it. Ancestors who lived in Canada prior to this establishment may have faced exclusion from citizenship. This historical context is crucial for understanding how citizenship has evolved and affected families over generations. Recent discussions and potential reforms aim to address these historical oversights, allowing those whose ancestors were denied citizenship due to these early rules to reclaim their status. The significance of 1947 continues to resonate in contemporary discussions regarding citizenship rules and lineage, underscoring the importance of recognizing historical injustices and finding ways to rectify them. As policymakers and advocates focus on these issues, there is hope for a more inclusive approach that honors the contributions of early residents and acknowledges their descendants' rightful claims to Canadian citizenship.

Canadian citizenship passes down through bloodlines. But if your ancestor lived in Canada before 1947, the rules of that era may have cut your family out of citizenship entirely—until now. item.title: Your Irish ancestors lived in Canada generations ago. You might still be Canadian
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Canada, Irish roots, ancestry, Cork, emigrants

Summary: In Canada, approximately 4.4 million people report having Irish roots, making Irish ancestry the third-largest in the country. This statistic reveals that nearly one in eight Canadians can trace their lineage back to Ireland, particularly to regions like Cork, Kerry, Wexford, and Tipperary. However, this number only accounts for those who remained in Canada. It does not reflect the many families who emigrated from Ireland over the years, leaving behind their roots for new opportunities. The cultural and historical connections between Canada and Ireland contribute to a rich tapestry of shared traditions and values. Recognizing both the presence of Irish descendants in Canada and the significance of those who migrated enhances understanding of the profound impact of Irish emigration on Canadian society. The legacy of Irish heritage continues to influence various aspects of life in Canada, from festivals and literature to culinary traditions, showcasing the enduring bond between the two nations. Thus, the story of Irish ancestry in Canada is not just about the living descendants but also about the memories and histories of those who crossed the ocean in search of a better life.

There are 4.4 million people in Canada who report Irish roots. item.title: Long-form birth certificates for Canadian citizenship by descent: what you need to know
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Canadian citizenship, ancestry, documentation, birth certificates, application

Summary: If you suspect you might be eligible for Canadian citizenship through your ancestry, it's essential to begin gathering the necessary documentation for your application to Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC). One of the primary documents you will need is the birth certificate. However, you may encounter situations where you have two different types of birth certificates - the short form and the long form. Understanding the difference between these forms is crucial, as the long-form birth certificate typically contains more detailed information, including parental details and place of birth. This comprehensive information can significantly strengthen your citizenship application. Additionally, ensure you have any other required documents related to your lineage, such as marriage certificates or proof of your ancestor's immigration status. By methodically collecting these documents and verifying their authenticity, you can facilitate a smoother application process. Once you have all your information organized, you can move forward confidently with your application to IRCC, increasing your chances of a successful outcome in your pursuit of Canadian citizenship through ancestry.

You’ve just realized that you might be eligible for Canadian citizenship based on your ancestry. **Keywords:** Canada, Express Entry, Australia, General Skilled Migration, Indian

Summary: Canada's Express Entry system and Australia's General Skilled Migration program are two of the most sought-after immigration pathways for skilled workers from India. Both systems facilitate the immigration of individuals based on their skills, qualifications, and work experience. The Express Entry system allows candidates to create an online profile and enter a pool of applicants, where they are ranked based on a points system. This system evaluates factors such as age, education, work experience, and language proficiency. Similarly, the General Skilled Migration program in Australia utilizes a points-based approach, with a focus on attracting skilled labor to meet labor market demands. Both programs have specific eligibility criteria and skill assessments that applicants must meet. The growing interest from Indian professionals in these immigration pathways is driven by the promise of better job opportunities, higher living standards, and the potential for permanent residency. Both Canada and Australia increasingly support skilled migrants, acknowledging their contributions to the economy and society. As a result, these programs continue to attract significant numbers of applicants from India each year.

item.title: Ontario has launched new PNP pathways - YouTube
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Express Entry, Canada Immigration, mistakes, IRCC, updates

Summary: The video by Sahil Sayal highlights the top 10 mistakes to avoid when applying for Canada’s Express Entry program during 2026-2027. Key points include the importance of thorough documentation, emphasizing that incomplete or inaccurate applications can lead to delays or rejections. Candidates should ensure they meet the eligibility criteria, particularly in areas such as language proficiency, which is critical for scoring well in the Comprehensive Ranking System (CRS). Utilizing accurate job descriptions that match the National Occupational Classification (NOC) is also stressed to avoid further complications. Sayal advises on maintaining up-to-date information on Express Entry draws and remaining vigilant about potential changes in immigration policies announced by IRCC. Networking and acquiring references can enhance one’s profile, while not understanding the significance of educational credential assessments (ECA) can undermine a candidate’s application. Financial readiness is underscored, highlighting that candidates need to demonstrate their ability to support themselves and their families upon arrival in Canada. The discussion concludes with a reminder to seek professional advice and remain informed about new developments in immigration procedures, as updates are frequent and impactful. Following these guidelines can facilitate a smoother application process and improve chances for successful immigration to Canada.

When a province nominates a person, that individual receives additional points in the Express Entry Comprehensive Ranking System (CRS), significantly enhancing their chances of receiving an Invitation to Apply (ITA) for PR. The nomination process typically involves the province assessing the applicant's skills, work experience, and ability to contribute to the local economy. Candidates may need to meet specific eligibility requirements set forth by the province, such as prior work experience in a particular field or skills in high-demand occupations. This targeted approach allows provinces to attract skilled workers who can fill gaps in their workforce, ultimately supporting regional economic growth. This shift could indicate the government's efforts to streamline the PR application process, providing a faster pathway for individuals seeking to settle permanently in Canada. For those interested in applying for work permits, ESDC encourages individuals to schedule a free consultation to understand their options better and navigate the complexities of the LMIA process. Summary: The Indian Express Group is a prominent media conglomerate in India, known for its diverse range of publications and news platforms. Key publications include The Indian Express, Loksatta, and Jansatta, catering to different linguistic and regional audiences. The group also operates online platforms like Inuth, expanding its digital footprint and engaging younger demographics. Another significant aspect of the group is the Ramnath Goenka Awards, which recognize excellence in journalism and contribute to the discourse around media integrity and independence. The Indian Express Group also publishes in regional languages, including IE Tamil, IE Malayalam, and IE Bangla, ensuring that it reaches a broader audience across India. This emphasis on regional content highlights the group's commitment to reflecting India's linguistic diversity and providing news that resonates with various communities. Overall, the Indian Express Group plays a crucial role in shaping public opinion and fostering informed discussions through its wide array of news offerings and recognition of journalistic excellence.

item.title: Processing time for in-Canada work permits falls to lowest this year
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Canada, immigration, processing times, work permits, Super visas

Summary: On June 24, Canada’s immigration department announced updated processing times for temporary residence applications, showcasing significant enhancements, especially for work permit applicants. The latest figures reveal a record low processing time for in-Canada work permits, now at just 144 days in 2026. This marks a substantial decrease, with processing times reducing by seven weeks for applicants from Nigeria. Additionally, Super visa processing times have improved considerably, reflecting the government's efforts to streamline the immigration process. Overall, these changes indicate a commitment to enhancing service efficiency and responsiveness to the needs of those seeking temporary residence in Canada. These improvements are expected to facilitate quicker and easier access for applicants, ultimately benefiting individuals and families looking to work or visit in Canada. The data reflects ongoing adjustments within the immigration department aimed at reducing backlog and optimizing the application process. The announcement is seen as a positive development in Canadian immigration strategy, potentially encouraging more applicants to consider Canada as their destination for work and family visits.

On June 24, Canada’s immigration department released updated processing times for temporary residence applications, showing significant improvements—particularly for work permit applicants. The most notable improvements since June 17 are as follows: Work permits: record low for in-Canada work permits (144 days) in 2026; down by seven weeks for Nigeria, Super visas: down by over a […]

item.title: Canada subjects immigration language tests to greater scrutiny
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: immigration, language test, CELPIP, fraudulent, instructions

Summary: On June 23, 2026, new instructions were issued to immigration officers, emphasizing a heightened vigilance against fraudulent language test results. As part of this directive, officers are now mandated to cross-reference the applicant's photographs to ensure the authenticity of the language test results. This measure aims to combat the increasing instances of fraud related to language proficiency certifications, which are critical for immigration applications. Additionally, the updated protocols require officers to undertake a comprehensive search of various records to verify the credibility of submitted tests. Applicants pursuing language assessments can book an Immigration Language Test with CELPIP, which is recognized widely for its rigor and reliability. The move to strengthen verification processes indicates a commitment to maintaining the integrity of the immigration system and ensuring that applicants meet the necessary language requirements. By implementing these measures, immigration authorities aim to create a more robust framework for evaluating language proficiency and to deter fraudulent activities that compromise the application process.

New instructions to immigration officers have put them on the lookout for fraudulent language test results. The updated instructions to officers, published online on June 23, 2026, require that officers cross-reference the applicant’s photographs to validate language test results. Book an Immigration Language Test with CELPIP Officers must now also perform an in-depth search of […]

item.title: Canada issues physicians invitations to apply for permanent residence
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Immigration, ITA, Express Entry, CRS score, Physicians

Summary: Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) recently issued additional Invitations to Apply (ITA) through the Express Entry system. In this particular draw, 271 ITAs were granted to candidates classified as Physicians with Canadian work experience. To qualify for this draw, candidates were required to meet certain criteria. Specifically, they needed to achieve a minimum Comprehensive Ranking System (CRS) score of 223. Additionally, candidates had to create their Express Entry profiles and submit their applications within the set timeframe. This targeted draw highlights the ongoing efforts by the IRCC to fill shortages in the healthcare sector by attracting qualified medical professionals to Canada. The Express Entry system continues to play a key role in managing the immigration process, enabling skilled workers to contribute to the Canadian economy and society. Moving forward, applicants in the Express Entry pool should be aware of the evolving criteria and remain prepared for potential future draws that may further impact their immigration opportunities in Canada.

item.title: Immigration minister says Canada is ‘not finalizing any new’ citizenship-by-descent applications, reviewing all files
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Canada, citizenship, Minister Lena Diab, descent files, halted decisions

Summary: On Tuesday, June 23, Canadian Immigration Minister Lena Diab announced that the government has paused all decisions related to citizenship by descent applications. This decision comes as part of a review of existing files, specifically targeting those who have already received citizenship certificates. Diab indicated that the review process is thorough, with officials going through the cases to determine their legitimacy and compliance with regulations. Individuals whose files are found to be in order are being informed that their citizenship status is secure. This move aims to address concerns regarding the integrity of the citizenship application process and ensure that only eligible candidates attain citizenship by descent. The Immigration Ministry is committed to upholding the legal framework governing citizenship in Canada, emphasizing the importance of a fair and transparent process. Those awaiting decisions on their applications might experience delays as the review unfolds, but the Ministry is focused on resolving these matters in a systematic and efficient manner to maintain public trust in the immigration system.

On Tuesday, June 23, Immigration Minister Lena Diab confirmed that Canada has halted decisions on citizenship by descent files, and is reviewing the files of people who have already been issued citizenship certificates. “Those that are deemed to be okay are being told [they’re] fine,” Diab said.  Get a Free Consultation on Applying for or Protecting […]

item.title: The Canadian citizenship rule most queer families don’t know exists
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Maya, Canadian citizenship, envelope, rejection, daughter

Summary: Maya, a resident of Sacramento, received an unexpected envelope from Nova Scotia this spring. Initially hesitant to open it due to her belief that it contained a rejection letter, she ultimately discovered it held a Canadian citizenship certificate for her four-year-old daughter. This surprising news opened up new possibilities for their family, highlighting a connection to Canadian heritage. The emergence of her daughter's citizenship could potentially affect their family's future decisions, including travel, education, and identity. Maya was prompted to reconsider the significance of her family's lineage and explore options for anyone interested in determining if their ancestry qualifies for citizenship as well. The article concludes with a call to action for readers to check their family lineage and to offer a free consultation service to help them navigate the process of acquiring citizenship based on their heritage. This story illustrates the emotional journey of navigating familial ties and legal documentation, emphasizing the importance of connection to one's roots and the benefits it may bring to future generations.

When the envelope from Nova Scotia landed in her Sacramento mailbox this spring, Maya* almost didn’t open it. She was convinced it would be a rejection. Inside was a proof of Canadian citizenship certificate. It had her four-year-old daughter’s name on it. Find out if your family lineage qualifies and get a free consultation with […]

item.title: Points for CoQ in Express Entry - YouTube
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: CoQ, Express Entry, Canada Immigration, Points, AHS Immigration

Summary: The Comprehensive Ranking System (CRS) in Canada’s Express Entry program assigns points based on various factors, including skills, work experience, and educational background. Obtaining a Certificate of Qualification (CoQ) can significantly enhance an applicant's profile by boosting their points in the Express Entry pool. This certificate demonstrates that the individual has met specific industry standards and possesses the necessary qualifications for their trade in Canada. Applicants aiming to increase their CRS score should consider obtaining a CoQ to improve their chances of receiving an Invitation to Apply (ITA) for permanent residency. It is important for potential immigrants to thoroughly research their eligibility for CoQ and prepare the required documentation. Additionally, consulting with immigration experts or firms, like AHS Immigration, can provide valuable insights and assistance throughout the application process. Overall, securing a CoQ not only strengthens a candidate's application but also provides greater opportunities for employment in skilled trades within Canada. Understanding the dynamics of the Express Entry system and capitalizing on helpful resources can lead to a successful immigration journey.

item.title: How Canada's skilled immigration system went off the rails – and how to fix it
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Express Entry, Canadian immigration, skilled labor, policy changes, immigration system

Summary: Express Entry was initially designed as a streamlined system to manage applications for Canadian skilled immigration, but its foundation was not solely focused on attracting skilled workers. Over the years, Express Entry has undergone significant transformations influenced by changes in labor market needs and political factors. The original intent was to create a fair and efficient process for immigrants, yet it has at times prioritized certain demographics and professions over others. This has led to an uneven impact on various sectors of the economy and has prompted critical discussions about the effectiveness of the immigration system. The program's structure has sparked debates regarding its accessibility and fairness, especially for skilled workers who may not fit into the narrow criteria established. As Canada continues to evolve its immigration policies, the future direction of Express Entry remains uncertain, with potential reforms aimed at addressing the emerging labor market demands and enhancing inclusivity. Ultimately, while Express Entry was not solely meant to serve as a Canadian skilled immigration program, it has become a pivotal element, reflecting the ongoing challenges and aspirations of Canada’s immigration landscape.
By Nino Melikidze and Steven Paolasini June 24, 2026. Express Entry was never meant to be a Canadian skilled immigration program.

item.title: Quebec announces July 2026 intake for family sponsorship, exempts adult dependent children from cap
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Quebec, family sponsorship, intake, applications, permanent residence

Summary: Quebec will re-open its intake for capped family sponsorship applications on July 2, 2026. Over a span of two years, the province will accept a maximum of 15,700 applications for individuals wishing to sponsor family members for permanent residence. The breakdown of these applications includes 13,300 specifically for spouses and 2,400 designated for parents or grandparents. Notably, the rules regarding dependent children have changed; dependent children aged 18 or older will no longer be subject to the same sponsorship restrictions as before. This adjustment aims to streamline the application process and address the needs of families wishing to reunite in Quebec. The provincial government is likely allowing this higher intake to accommodate the significant demand for family reunification within the province. Overall, this initiative reflects Quebec's ongoing commitment to fostering family ties and supporting its residents in bringing their loved ones to join them in Canada. Potential sponsors and applicants are encouraged to prepare for the intake period to ensure timely submission of their applications for consideration.

Quebec will re-open intake for capped family sponsorship applications on July 2, 2026. Over a two-year period, the province will accept a maximum of 15,700 applications for undertakings to sponsor family members for permanent residence: 13,300 for spouses, and 2,400 for parents or grandparents. Dependent children aged 18 or older will no longer be subject […]

item.title: Express Entry pool gets more top heavy
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Express Entry, profiles, candidates, growth rate, competitive

Summary: From May 24 to June 21, the trends in the Express Entry system revealed significant growth in top-ranking profiles. The overall pool experienced a modest increase of 0.33%, illustrating the gradual rise of candidates within the system. In sharp contrast, the number of candidates scoring 501 or higher surged impressively, escalating by 14.64%. This dramatic uptick indicates a competitive edge among individuals aiming for high-ranking profiles in the Express Entry system, showcasing a growth rate that is 4,400% higher than the overall pool. Such statistics reflect a shifting dynamic in the labor market and immigration landscape, as more candidates strive to enhance their scores and improve their chances for selection. The focus on scores within the 501–1,200 range suggests a nuanced competition, emphasizing the importance for prospective candidates to evaluate their own profiles and understand the criteria that distinguish top contenders. By assessing their competitiveness, individuals may strategize effectively to bolster their positions within the Express Entry framework, positioning themselves favorably in the selection process for permanent residency in Canada.

From May 24 to June 21, the number of top-scoring Express Entry profiles grew at a rate 4,400% faster than the overall pool. The overall Express Entry pool grew by 0.33%, while the number of candidates scoring 501 or higher increased by 14.64%. This draw identified candidates who possess a minimum Comprehensive Ranking System (CRS) score of 516. To be eligible for consideration in this round, candidates had to ensure that their Express Entry profiles were created prior to the draw. The issuance of these ITAs reflects the government's ongoing focus on attracting skilled individuals with Canadian experience, which plays a critical role in meeting labor market demands across the country. This latest round of invitations underscores the importance of the Express Entry system in Canada’s immigration strategy, enabling qualified candidates to seek permanent residency in the nation. Candidates aiming for success in future draws are encouraged to enhance their CRS scores through various means, such as improving language proficiency, gaining additional work experience, or obtaining a provincial nomination, which can significantly boost their chances of receiving an ITA. Overall, the draw is a key step for many aspiring immigrants looking to contribute to Canadian society.

item.title: 10 Proven Resume Tips for Immigrants (and CELPIP test takers) to Canada That Can Accelerate Your Job Search Success
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Canada, job search, skilled immigrants, qualifications, challenges

Summary: Moving to Canada presents an exciting opportunity for skilled immigrants, but the journey to securing a first job can be daunting. Many newcomers arrive with exceptional qualifications and extensive work experience, yet they often face significant challenges in obtaining interview calls. One key factor that contributes to this difficulty is the importance of effective communication skills, particularly conversational English. It is crucial for immigrants to practice their language proficiency, not only for the CELPIP test but also for job interviews, as clear communication can significantly impact their chances of being hired. Additionally, understanding the job market landscape, networking, and tailoring resumes to meet Canadian standards are essential steps for improving job prospects. Overcoming barriers, such as unfamiliarity with local hiring practices and cultural differences, is vital for professionals seeking employment in their respective fields. By addressing these challenges head-on, skilled immigrants can enhance their chances of success and make a smoother transition into the Canadian workforce.

Moving to Canada is an exciting opportunity, but finding your first job can be one of the biggest challenges. Many skilled immigrants arrive with impressive qualifications and years of experience, yet struggle to get interview calls (remember to practice your conversational English, for your CELPIP test as well as your job interviews!). item.title: Federal–Provincial–Territorial Immigration Ministers meet to discuss sustainable immigration
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: FMRI, Immigration Levels Plan, Provincial Nominee Programs, labour shortages, PNP allocations

Summary: The Forum of Ministers Responsible for Immigration (FMRI) convened to discuss the 2027–2029 Immigration Levels Plan and to foster collaboration on federal–provincial–territorial (FPT) priorities. A significant focus of the meeting was on returning immigration to sustainable levels, taking into account various regional, rural, and northern contexts. The ministers acknowledged the pressing need to address labour shortages that affect key economic sectors across Canada. They emphasized the importance of supporting communities nationwide in their immigration strategies. Additionally, the role of Provincial Nominee Programs (PNPs) was highlighted as crucial in meeting these goals. The ministers discussed the necessity for stable and adequate allocations of PNPs to enhance their effectiveness in addressing local labour market needs. Overall, the meeting aimed to align immigration goals with the economic and demographic realities of different regions, ensuring a balanced approach that benefits all Canadians. The discussions will help shape a collaborative framework for future immigration policies that respond to both national needs and local conditions.
The Forum of Ministers Responsible for Immigration (FMRI) met today to advance collaboration on the 2027–2029 Immigration Levels Plan and discuss federal–provincial–territorial (FPT) priorities. They discussed returning immigration to sustainable levels while considering regional, rural and northern contexts, addressing labour shortages, reinforcing key economic sectors, and supporting communities across the country. Ministers also discussed the role of Provincial Nominee Programs (PNPs) in advancing these considerations and the need for stable and adequate PNP allocations.

The ministers emphasized the necessity of improving newcomer outcomes by facilitating better integration into the community and job market. This includes providing adequate support services and resources to help newcomers settle and thrive. Recommendations were made to streamline application processes and enhance communication between federal and provincial governments to achieve these goals. The PT ministers underscored the vital role that immigration plays in rejuvenating local economies and supporting demographic stability. They collectively called for greater flexibility within the Express Entry system that would allow provinces to attract immigrants who meet their unique labour market needs. Overall, the discussions aimed at not only improving the immigration system but also ensuring that it effectively benefits both newcomers and the communities they enter.

item.content:encoded:
Overview, Express Entry, Skilled Immigration, CRS, Draws 2026

Summary: The Express Entry system is a pathway for skilled immigrants to become permanent residents in Canada. It operates through a points-based Comprehensive Ranking System (CRS) that assesses candidates based on factors like age, education, work experience, and language proficiency. The system periodically conducts draws, inviting the highest-ranking candidates to apply for permanent residency. In 2026, additional draws may take place, providing more opportunities for skilled workers. This approach aims to meet labor market needs and support economic growth. Candidates are encouraged to improve their CRS scores by obtaining higher language test results, additional education, or obtaining work experience, thereby enhancing their chances of being selected in future draws. The Express Entry program continues to evolve, adapting to immigration goals, labor shortages, and demographic changes in Canada. Understanding the nuances of the CRS and staying updated on draw results and criteria are essential for prospective applicants to navigate the immigration process successfully. As the program develops, it remains a vital tool for both Canada and skilled workers seeking to contribute to the country’s economy and society.

Summary: The number of top-scoring Express Entry profiles experienced an unprecedented growth rate of 4,400% from May 24 to June 21, significantly outpacing the overall pool of profiles. This surge indicates a substantial increase in high-ranking candidates for immigration under Canada's Express Entry system. While the overall pool also saw growth, it occurred at a much slower pace, highlighting the competitive nature of the immigration landscape. The rise in top-scoring profiles may point to increased interest in Canadian immigration programs or possibly a response to changes in immigration policies during that period. Additionally, this trend could affect application processing times and selection criteria, potentially benefiting skilled workers and experienced professionals looking to settle in Canada. The dramatic shift in profile numbers suggests that both candidates and immigration authorities should prepare for further changes and trends in the coming months. Overall, the rapid growth of top-scoring profiles confirms the dynamic nature of the Express Entry system and remains an important area for future monitoring by prospective immigrants and stakeholders in the immigration sector.

item.title: Express Entry CEC Draw of 4000- CRS Drops to 516 - YouTube
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: CEC draw, Invitations to Apply, IRCC, CRS score, 4000

Summary: The Canadian Experience Class (CEC) draw is proceeding as planned, with the latest round resulting in the issuance of 4,000 Invitations to Apply (ITAs) for permanent residency. This draw reflects the ongoing commitment of the Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) to facilitate immigration and address labor shortages in the country. The Comprehensive Ranking System (CRS) score in the latest draw decreased to 516, down from 518 in the previous round. This minor drop in score indicates a slight easing of competition among candidates, potentially allowing more individuals to qualify for immigration. Such draws are crucial for maintaining a steady influx of skilled workers, which is vital for Canada's economic growth and recovery, especially post-pandemic. The IRCC's approach underscores the importance of attracting talent with Canadian work experience, thereby benefiting both the immigrants and the Canadian economy. As immigration policies continue to evolve, prospective applicants are encouraged to stay informed about upcoming draws and changes in the CRS scoring system, maximizing their chances to secure an ITA in future rounds.

Summary: Canada has updated its police certificate requirements for foreign nationals applying for work permits through the International Experience Canada (IEC) program. The changes reflect new instructions issued to immigration officers, which can be found on the immigration department's official website. One key aspect of these updated guidelines is the need for a police certificate from the applicant's current country of residence. This clarification aims to streamline the application process and ensure that applicants present the necessary documentation. The updated requirements are intended to enhance security measures and maintain the integrity of the work permit application process. As applicants prepare for their IEC applications, they are advised to be aware of these changes and ensure they have the proper police verification documentation in place. This move is part of Canada's effort to facilitate international mobility while ensuring safety and compliance with immigration regulations.

item.title: How a Canadian great-grandparent can make you a citizen
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Canadian, citizen, great-grandparent, apply, passport

Summary: If one of your great-grandparents was a Canadian citizen, you may qualify for Canadian citizenship regardless of where you, your parents, or your grandparents were born. This allows individuals who have no recent ties to Canada, such as holding a Canadian passport in generations, the opportunity to connect with their heritage. To gain citizenship, you need to establish your relationship with your Canadian great-grandparent through appropriate documentation. This process can provide you with the chance to become a Canadian citizen and access the rights and privileges associated with it. Once you gather the necessary proof of lineage, you can proceed to apply for citizenship. Being aware of this possibility can open doors for individuals seeking to explore their family history and gain Canadian nationality, thus potentially benefiting from Canada's resources, services, and community as citizens. This citizenship pathway emphasizes the significance of familial connections and may be an avenue for many who wish to embrace their Canadian roots.

If one of your great-grandparents was Canadian, you may now be a Canadian citizen—even if you, your parents, and your grandparents were all born abroad, and no one in the family has held a Canadian passport in generations. If you can prove your relation to your Canadian great-grandparent, you can apply for the document that […]

This draw was significant as it opened opportunities for individuals looking to immigrate to Canada under the PNP pathway. To be eligible for this draw, candidates were required to have a minimum Comprehensive Ranking System (CRS) score of 730, which ensures that only those with higher qualifications and potential for success in Canada were considered. The Express Entry system is a vital component of Canada’s immigration strategy, aiming to attract skilled workers to meet labor market demands. It not only facilitates application processing but also allows for a more streamlined immigration experience. This latest draw highlights the ongoing commitment of the IRCC to invite skilled individuals and support provincial economies by filling labor shortages. Candidates who meet the criteria and receive an ITA will have the opportunity to apply for permanent residency in Canada, marking a significant step toward building their future in the country.

item.title: Canada gives second chance to asylum seekers from Uganda
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Uganda, Canada, protected person status, negative decision, measures

Summary: On June 19, new measures were announced that will benefit Ugandan nationals and former residents of Uganda facing imminent removal from Canada. The initiative aims to provide these individuals with a second chance to obtain protected person status. Specifically, it applies to those who received a final negative decision regarding their application between June 20, 2025, and June 19, 2026. This move is part of Canada's efforts to address the unique challenges and risks faced by Ugandans, particularly given the political and social climate in Uganda. Eligible individuals will have the opportunity to reapply and present their cases for consideration under these new regulations. This initiative reflects Canada's commitment to providing protection and support to individuals in need, ensuring that those who may have legitimate claims to refugee status are not unfairly removed from the country. The measures demonstrate the government’s responsive approach to changing circumstances and underlying human rights issues in Uganda, reaffirming Canada’s role in global humanitarian efforts.

Many individuals from Uganda who had faced imminent removal from Canada will be given a second chance at obtaining protected person status. item.title: How to request urgent processing of a Canadian citizenship certificate
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: proof of citizenship, IRCC, processing time, Bill C-3, Canadian

Summary: A proof of citizenship certificate is an important document for confirming Canadian citizenship. The processing time for this certificate through Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) currently stands at approximately 15 months. This lengthy wait time has increased recently due to a surge in applications. A significant number of applicants are American citizens who have become newly eligible for Canadian citizenship under Bill C-3, which took effect in December 2025. As a result, the demand for proof of citizenship has escalated, leading to a growing queue of applicants. The influx of applications reflects the changing landscape of citizenship eligibility in Canada, particularly driven by recent legislative changes. Individuals seeking to verify their Canadian citizenship are now facing a much longer waiting period, which highlights the pressing need for IRCC to address processing delays and improve service efficiency. Overall, the situation emphasizes the importance of being informed about the citizenship application process and the potential timelines involved.

A proof of citizenship certificate is the document that confirms you’re already Canadian. IRCC currently takes about 15 months to process one, and that wait has climbed as hundreds of thousands of people, many of them Americans newly eligible under Bill C-3, have joined the queue since the law took effect in December 2025. For […]

Summary: Temporary residents in Canada are currently evaluating their pathways to permanent residency and may find that provincial nomination programs (PNPs) are more advantageous than the federal Express Entry system at this time. Recent trends indicate that the transition from temporary resident (TR) to permanent resident (PR) has shown a significant flow through PNPs, especially as provinces are actively seeking to attract and retain skilled workers. Many provinces have launched specific streams targeting temporary residents, offering them tailored opportunities that align with local labor market needs. The processing times for PNP applications can sometimes be quicker than Express Entry, making PNPs an attractive option for those looking to secure permanent residency efficiently. Additionally, candidates with provincial nomination can receive enhanced points in the Express Entry system, benefiting their overall applications. Therefore, those on temporary permits, such as work or study permits, should closely examine the PNP options available in their province, as these may provide a more streamlined and effective routing to permanent residency compared to the federated system's requirements. item.content:encoded:

Summary: Recently, Canada's citizenship department requested some newly naturalized Canadians to surrender their citizenship certificates for review due to flagged applications. This decision caused uncertainty among the affected individuals, who were concerned about their status as citizens. However, less than a week later, in a surprising turnaround, the government began notifying many of those individuals that they could retain their citizenship certificates. This quick reversal has led to some confusion among applicants, emphasizing the government's reassessment of the situation. The citizenship department's initial request was likely driven by concerns over the integrity of some applications, but the subsequent decision to issue "revalidation" for certain individuals suggests that many may not have been affected as initially thought. This incident highlights the complexities and challenges that can arise in the citizenship application process, especially when it comes to ensuring eligibility and integrity among applicants. Ultimately, the swift change in direction aims to restore confidence in the citizenship process while addressing the concerns of those newly naturalized Canadians who were impacted by the initial request.

Candidates who receive these letters are given the opportunity to proceed with their applications for permanent residency, emphasizing the importance of having a valid Express Entry profile. This draw demonstrates the Canadian government's ongoing commitment to facilitating immigration pathways for skilled professionals, ultimately contributing to the country's economic growth and diversity. The recent issuance of Letters of Advice aligns with a broader trend of targeted immigration policies that seek to balance labor market needs with demographic growth, thereby enhancing Canada’s workforce and global competitiveness.

To be eligible for this draw, candidates needed to have created their Express Entry profiles by 2:52:14 AM UTC on the specified date in November. The timing of profile creation is critical, as only those profiles submitted before this cutoff were considered for the draw. The CRS score of 521 indicates a competitive selection process, typically reflecting the qualifications, work experience, and skills of the candidates. This draw underscores the importance of staying updated and prepared for potential applicants looking to immigrate to Canada through the Express Entry system. Overall, the CEC draw represents a significant opportunity for skilled workers to transition into permanent residency in Canada, contributing to the nation’s vibrant workforce.

The lack of a point-based evaluation means that evaluations might focus on other criteria, perhaps emphasizing practical skills or qualifications rather than numerical scores alone. item.title: Canada moves to enshrine early access to work permits for asylum seekers
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Ottawa, asylum claimants, work permits, Immigration, Regulations

Summary: On June 19, 2026, Ottawa announced plans to permanently allow asylum claimants faster access to work permits through proposed changes to the Immigration and Refugee Protection Regulations. This initiative aims to streamline the process for asylum seekers, providing them with the opportunity to work while their claims are being processed. Currently, the regulations impose specific conditions on when an asylum seeker is eligible for a work permit. The move reflects the government’s recognition of the need to support individuals seeking refuge and to improve their integration into Canadian society. By expediting work permits, the government hopes to alleviate some of the economic burdens faced by asylum seekers, enabling them to contribute to the economy more rapidly. The proposed changes were made public in the Canada Gazette, indicating the government's commitment to enhancing the immigration system. Stakeholders and advocacy groups are expected to provide feedback on these changes, as the government seeks to balance security concerns with humanitarian considerations. Overall, this shift represents a significant step towards a more compassionate approach to asylum seekers in Canada, aligning with the country’s values of inclusivity and community support.

Ottawa has moved to make permanent the ability for asylum claimants to obtain faster access to work permits. The move comes in the form of draft changes to the Immigration and Refugee Protection Regulations, published in the Canada Gazette the afternoon of June 19, 2026. These programs are designed to attract skilled workers and support immigration to the province. Candidates may assess their eligibility for these enhanced Provincial Nominee Program (PNP) streams to determine if they qualify for the opportunities offered. The selection round reflects New Brunswick's ongoing efforts to address labor shortages and bolster its economy by inviting individuals who can contribute to its workforce. This recent influx of invitations underscores the province's commitment to welcoming immigrants as part of its broader immigration strategy. Those interested in living and working in New Brunswick are encouraged to review the eligibility criteria for the various streams available under the NBPNP and AIP to better understand their potential immigration pathways.

item.title: Canada just changed what counts as proof of citizenship by descent — here’s the breakdown
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Canada, citizenship, descent, documentation, lineage

Summary: Canada's citizenship department has introduced new guidance regarding the proof of Canadian lineage required from individuals applying for citizenship by descent. This change follows instances where multiple new citizens were asked to return their citizenship certificates due to insufficient documentation. The updated guidelines have raised the bar for the supporting documentation necessary in citizenship-by-descent applications, which now require a more rigorous documentary standard. This initiative aims to ensure the integrity of the citizenship process and to prevent fraudulent claims. As a result of these changes, applicants may face stricter scrutiny of their lineage proof, leading to possible adjustments in how they prepare their documentation. The citizenship department is emphasizing thoroughness and accuracy in applications to uphold the process's credibility. These modifications reflect the government’s commitment to ensuring that citizenship rights are granted only to those with verified Canadian ties, thereby reinforcing the nation's citizenship policies. The implications of this new guidance may impact many applicants and necessitate a reevaluation of how they present their cases. Overall, the update signifies a shift toward more stringent verification standards in Canada’s citizenship by descent framework.

Canada’s citizenship department has issued new guidance on what it expects as proof of Canadian lineage from people applying for citizenship by descent, after asking multiple new Canadian citizens to return their citizenship certificates based on their submitted documentation. These changes raise the accepted supporting documentation and the documentary standard for citizenship-by-descent applications. Among other […]

item.title: Canada pauses processing of some citizenship-by-descent applications, clarifies rules for those under review
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Canada, immigration, citizenship, applications, citizenship-by-descent

Summary: Canada's immigration department has halted the finalization of certain citizenship-by-descent applications. This decision comes after several individuals, who had already received approval for their citizenship, received letters instructing them to surrender their citizenship certificates. The government is currently re-examining these applications, suggesting potential issues with the approval process or applicants' eligibility. The pause affects a limited number of cases, but it raises concerns about the overall integrity of the citizenship-by-descent process. People affected by this situation may face uncertainty regarding their citizenship status and future applications. The immigration department has not yet released a timeline for how long the review will take or the reasons behind the actions taken. Individuals considering applying for citizenship-by-descent may want to seek legal advice or consultation to understand the implications of this change and how it may affect their applications. As more information becomes available, it will be crucial for applicants to stay informed about the status of the citizenship process in Canada. The situation highlights the complexities and evolving nature of immigration policies in the country.

Canada’s immigration department has temporarily stopped finalizing some new citizenship-by-descent applications, according to reporting by Global News and The Canadian Press. The pause follows letters sent to a few dozen people who had already been approved, telling them to surrender their citizenship certificates while their files are re-examined. Overall, the initiative reflects the province's commitment to enhancing its immigration system to accommodate and welcome newcomers more effectively. item.title: The Bill C-3 paradox: Millions now qualify for Canadian citizenship, but few will apply
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: United States, Canada, citizenship, second passport, Americans

Summary: The article discusses a little-known aspect of Canada's citizenship law, which allows many Americans living in the United States to claim Canadian citizenship without realizing it. This new law suggests that these individuals may already be citizens based on specific criteria, such as having a Canadian parent or grandparent. The process requires them to gather certain documents and follow a series of steps to prove their Canadian heritage. By doing so, they could potentially obtain a second citizenship, gain access to a Canadian passport, and enjoy the rights and privileges that come with being a citizen of Canada. This revelation is significant as it implies that millions of people may unknowingly have dual citizenship, which may provide them with more opportunities and benefits in areas such as healthcare, education, and travel. The article aims to raise awareness among Americans about their eligibility and encourages them to explore their familial connections to Canada and take action if they choose to obtain their second citizenship. Overall, it highlights an important and often overlooked opportunity for many in the U.S. to connect with their Canadian roots.

The United States is now home to millions of secret Canadians who have no idea that they are currently eligible for a second citizenship, passport, and all the rights that come with it. Under Canada’s new citizenship law, these Americans are already citizens; they just need to follow the trail of clues to prove it […]

item.title: Canada moved the goalposts for proof of citizenship applicants, lawyers say
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Canadian citizenship, descent, certificates, surrender, regulations

Summary: On June 13, 2026, the Registrar of Citizenship sent letters to an undisclosed number of Canadian citizens by descent, instructing them to surrender their citizenship certificates. This directive was issued under the authority of Citizenship Regulations 26(1), indicating potential infractions concerning the rules of citizenship that the recipients claim were never disclosed to them. The affected individuals may find themselves in a distressing situation, as they are being asked to relinquish proof of their citizenship status without prior knowledge of any violations on their part. The implications of this enforcement raise questions about transparency in communication from the Canadian government and the processes involved in determining adherence to citizenship regulations. The citizenship by descent process is designed to ensure that individuals born outside Canada but with Canadian parentage can claim their citizenship. However, the recent actions suggest systemic issues that could affect many individuals, undermining their rights and sense of security regarding their citizenship status. As this situation unfolds, it will be essential to monitor how the government addresses these concerns and whether any recourse will be available for those impacted by the requested surrender of their certificates.

Canadian citizenship certificate holders have been instructed to surrender their certificates for having broken rules they were never told. On June 13, 2026, an unknown number of Canadian citizen by descent certificate holders received letters from the Registrar of Citizenship requiring them to surrender their certificates, under the authority of Citizenship Regulations 26(1), as their […]

item.content:encoded:

Summary: On June 17, Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) updated its processing time estimates for various temporary residence categories. The update revealed shorter wait times for some applicants, reflecting improvements in processing efficiency. Specifically, in-Canada work permit timelines show significant progress, with a decrease of over two weeks in wait times for applicants. Additionally, submissions for work permits from India and Nigeria have seen a reduction in processing times as well, with one-week decreases reported for those applications. This positive trend in processing times suggests a growing capacity within the IRCC to handle applications more effectively. The reduction in wait times is likely to benefit many prospective applicants seeking temporary residence in Canada, making the transition smoother and more efficient. Overall, the updated processing times signal the IRCC’s commitment to enhancing service delivery and responding to the needs of applicants engaged in Temporary Residence programs.

item.title: Forced surrender of Canadian citizenship certificates may be unconstitutional, experts say
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Canadian citizenship, constitutional rights, Registrar, surrender, investigation

Summary: On June 13, 2026, the Canadian government, through the Registrar of Citizenship, issued mass letters to individuals born outside Canada. These letters demanded the immediate surrender of their Canadian citizenship certificates. This action was taken while the cases of these individuals were being investigated, raising serious concerns about potential violations of constitutional rights. Many holders of proof of Canadian citizenship certificates felt alarmed and confused by this unexpected requirement. The government's abrupt move has led to widespread outrage and calls for clarification regarding the legal justification behind the mass investigation and potential repercussions for those affected. Critics argue that the measure undermines the trust citizens have in governmental processes, particularly in matters concerning their citizenship status. Legal experts are closely watching the situation, questioning the constitutionality of such a sweeping demand without due process. Individuals impacted by this order have expressed fears of loss of identity and legal rights, urging for immediate legal recourse and assurances from government officials. Overall, this unprecedented action by the federal government has ignited a debate about citizenship rights and the need for safeguarding individual freedoms in Canada.

Holders of proof of Canadian citizenship certificates may have had their constitutional rights violated by the federal government. On June 13, 2026, the Registrar of citizenship issued letters en masse to persons who had been born outside Canada, requiring the immediate surrender of their Canadian citizenship certificates while their cases were being investigated, as authorized […]

The post Forced surrender of Canadian citizenship certificates may be unconstitutional, experts say first appeared on CIC News.

item.title: IRCC’s total application backlog drops to lowest level since July 2025
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: immigration, backlog, IRCC, Express Entry, decrease

Summary: The immigration department's total application backlog has decreased significantly, with April 2026 figures indicating it is at its lowest since July 2025. Noteworthy changes highlighted in the Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) update include a record low backlog of 9% specifically for the Express Entry program. Additionally, there has been a 5% decrease in the overall backlog, reflecting the government's ongoing efforts to streamline immigration processing and reduce waiting times for applicants. This continuous decline in backlog levels suggests progress in addressing previous delays and improving the efficiency of the immigration system. As a result, applicants may experience a more timely resolution of their immigration status. Overall, these updates signify a positive trend for IRCC as it works to enhance its operations and support prospective immigrants while responding to the growing demand for immigration services. The current state of the backlog underscores a commitment to improving processes and welcoming new residents more effectively.
